name: Covaleda Waterfalls Trail
name_original: Cascadas de Covaleda – trail running circular
description: Run the Cascadas de Covaleda circular trail, a difficult 4.4-mile route through pine forests to multiple waterfalls.
region_intro: Jogging routes around Covaleda offer an exceptional environment for outdoor activity, set within the vast Pinares de Urbión, Europe's largest pine forest. The region is characterized by its dramatic mountain scenery, including the Sierra de Urbión and its peaks, providing challenging inclines and rewarding views. Numerous glacial lagoons and the Duero River, with its waterfalls and historic bridges, add diverse natural features to the running landscape. This combination of dense forests, mountainous terrain, and water features creates varied running experiences. Best jogging routes around Covaleda The most popular jogging route is Pico Urbión Loop from Laguna Negra, an 8.3 miles (13.4 km) trail that takes 2 hours 37 minutes to complete. This difficult route offers panoramic views towards Pico de Urbión and passes by glacial lagoons. Another top favourite among local runners is Covaleda Waterfalls Trail, a difficult 4.4 miles (7.0 km) path. This route features dynamic natural landmarks like the Paso Peñoncito Waterfall and the Ojeda River Waterfall. Local runners also love the Castroviejo and Cueva Serena Trail, a 4.4 miles (7.1 km) trail leading through dense pine forests and unique rock formations, often completed in about 1 hour 5 minutes. Jogging around Covaleda is defined by extensive pine forests, dramatic mountain landscapes, and scenic river paths with waterfalls. The network offers options for various ability levels, from moderate forest runs to difficult mountain ascents. The routes in Covaleda are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 5.0 stars from 1 review. More than 200 runners have used komoot to explore Covaleda's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on the running trails around Covaleda?
A: The running trails in Covaleda offer a diverse range of terrain. You'll find yourself navigating through the vast Pinares de Urbión, Europe's largest pine forest, which provides shaded paths and a fresh atmosphere. Many routes also feature dramatic mountain scenery, including challenging inclines towards peaks like Pico de Urbión, and paths around stunning glacial lagoons. Additionally, you can expect scenic river paths with waterfalls and historic bridges, such as those along the Duero River. This mix of dense forests, mountainous areas, and water features ensures a varied and engaging running experience.

Q: Are there any easy running routes suitable for beginners in Covaleda?
A: While many of the routes around Covaleda are rated as difficult due to their elevation and technical sections, the region does offer options for various ability levels. The extensive pine forests provide numerous paths that can be enjoyed at a more moderate pace. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1064167423/castroviejo-and-cueva-serena-trail" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Castroviejo and Cueva Serena Trail&lt;/a&gt;, though rated difficult, is a shorter 7.1 km route through dense pine forests and unique rock formations, which might be a good starting point for those looking for a less strenuous option compared to mountain ascents.

Q: What are some scenic points of interest I can see while jogging in Covaleda?
A: Covaleda's running routes are rich with natural and historical landmarks. You can encounter stunning waterfalls like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4704240" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ojeda River Waterfall&lt;/a&gt; and &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4704239" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Paso Peñoncito Waterfall&lt;/a&gt;, especially on routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1064189817/covaleda-waterfalls-trail" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Covaleda Waterfalls Trail&lt;/a&gt;. The region also boasts several beautiful glacial lagoons, such as &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3844856" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Laguna Larga&lt;/a&gt; and the famous &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/6224900" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Laguna Negra&lt;/a&gt;. Historic bridges, including medieval and Romanesque structures, also add a cultural dimension to the natural routes.

Q: Are there any circular running routes in the Covaleda area?
A: Yes, Covaleda offers several excellent circular running routes. For instance, the challenging &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1064155512/pico-urbion-loop-from-laguna-negra" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Pico Urbión Loop from Laguna Negra&lt;/a&gt; is an 8.3-mile trail that provides panoramic views. Another option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1064168646/mirador-de-castroviejo-cascada-de-la-chorla-y-cueva-serena-trail-running-circular-desde-duruelo-de-la-sierra" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Mirador de Castroviejo, cascada de la Chorla y Cueva Serena – trail running circular desde Duruelo de la Sierra&lt;/a&gt;, which is a longer 15 km loop. These circular paths allow you to experience diverse landscapes without retracing your steps.

Q: What do other runners enjoy most about the trails in Covaleda?
A: The running trails in Covaleda are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 5.0 stars. Runners frequently praise the region's breathtaking natural landscapes, the invigorating atmosphere of Europe's largest pine forest, and the variety of experiences offered by the dramatic mountain scenery, glacial lagoons, and picturesque waterfalls. The extensive network of trails caters to different preferences, from shaded forest runs to challenging mountain ascents, making it a favorite among outdoor enthusiasts.

Q: Can I bring my dog on the running trails in Covaleda?
A: Covaleda's natural environment is generally welcoming for dogs, but it's always recommended to check specific regulations for each trail or protected area. Given the region's focus on preserving its natural environment within the Sierra de Urbión and Pinares de Urbión, it's advisable to keep dogs on a leash, especially in areas with wildlife or near livestock. Always practice responsible pet ownership by cleaning up after your dog.

Q: Are there any family-friendly running routes in Covaleda?
A: While many routes are rated difficult, the vast network of paths through the Pinares de Urbión offers gentler sections that can be suitable for families looking for a lighter jog or walk. Look for trails that stick to the lower elevations within the pine forests, which provide shaded and relatively flat terrain. Always consider the distance and elevation gain when choosing a route for families, and ensure everyone is comfortable with the chosen path.

Q: What is the best time of year to go running in Covaleda?
A: Covaleda offers a great running experience throughout much of the year. Spring and autumn are particularly pleasant, with mild temperatures and vibrant natural colors. The dense pine forests provide welcome shade during warmer summer months, making it possible to run even then. Winter running is also an option, though some higher elevation routes, especially towards Pico de Urbión and glacial lagoons, might be challenging due to snow and ice, requiring appropriate gear and caution.

Q: Are permits required to access any of the running trails in Covaleda?
A: Generally, permits are not required for most running trails in the Covaleda area, especially those within the extensive Pinares de Urbión. However, if you plan to venture into specific protected natural parks or reserves, or access certain sensitive areas, it's always a good practice to check with local authorities or park information centers for any specific regulations or permit requirements that might apply. For the routes listed, no specific permits are mentioned as being required.

Q: How many running routes are available around Covaleda?
A: There are over 30 dedicated running routes available around Covaleda on komoot, offering a wide range of distances and difficulties. These routes allow you to explore the diverse landscapes of the region, from the vast pine forests to the dramatic mountain scenery and picturesque river paths.

Q: Where can I find parking for the running trails in Covaleda?
A: Parking availability varies depending on the specific trailhead. Many popular routes and natural attractions in Covaleda, especially those leading to areas like Castroviejo, the waterfalls, or towards the Sierra de Urbión, often have designated parking areas nearby. It's advisable to check the starting point of your chosen route on komoot or local maps for specific parking information. For example, routes starting near Refugio de Pescadores or the village of Covaleda itself usually have accessible parking.

Q: Are there any running routes that feature historical bridges?
A: Yes, the Covaleda region is home to several ancient bridges that add a historical touch to its natural routes. The Duero River flows through Covaleda, and you can find routes that pass by structures like the Soria Bridge and the Santo Domingo Bridge. These bridges, some medieval or Romanesque, offer scenic spots and a unique blend of nature and history for your run.
tips: The last of the 3 waterfalls on the Route of the Covaleda Waterfalls. The descent can be somewhat tricky and/or technical, so I recommend wearing the correct footwear and being careful when slipping. Once down we can see a beautiful waterfall that we will surely want to photograph.

The second of the 3 waterfalls is the Cascada de la Chorrera, as in the other 3, the descent can be somewhat complicated and/or technical, so I recommend wearing the correct footwear and being careful when slipping. Once down we can see a beautiful waterfall that we will surely want to photograph.
The Paso Peñoncito Waterfall is the first one we find going up but I recommend that we visit it last, since the most common route continues from the base of this waterfall.

faqs.question: What is the overall difficulty of the Cascadas de Covaleda trail for trail running?
faqs.answer: Komoot rates this trail as difficult for trail running. While the overall length and elevation gain might seem moderate, some sections, especially the descents to the waterfalls, can be steep and potentially slippery. These parts require caution and good footing, making it a more challenging experience for runners.

faqs.question: How long does it typically take to complete the Cascadas de Covaleda trail running circular route?
faqs.answer: For jogging, the route is estimated to take around 1 hour and 3 minutes. If you're walking at a more relaxed pace, perhaps enjoying the scenery and waterfalls, it generally takes between 2 to 3 hours.

faqs.question: What kind of terrain can I expect on this trail?
faqs.answer: The trail primarily consists of paths and some tracks, winding through beautiful, dense forests of pine, oak, holly, and beech. You'll experience sections that feel like a 'tunnel of trees.' Be aware that some parts, particularly those leading down to the waterfalls, can be steep and potentially slippery, though wooden steps and handrails are present in some areas to assist.

faqs.question: What are the main natural features and highlights along the route?
faqs.answer: The main draw of this circular route is its succession of stunning waterfalls. You'll encounter the Ojeda River Waterfall, Chorrera Waterfall, and Paso Peñoncito Waterfall. The trail also passes through rich forest ecosystems within the picturesque Sierra de Urbión.

faqs.question: Is the Cascadas de Covaleda trail dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: Yes, the trail is generally dog-friendly. However, given the potentially steep and slippery sections near the waterfalls, it's advisable to keep your dog on a leash and ensure they are comfortable with varied terrain. Always practice responsible pet ownership and clean up after your dog.

faqs.question: Do I need any permits or pay an entrance fee to access the trail or the Sierra de Urbión area?
faqs.answer: No, there are typically no permits or entrance fees required to access the Cascadas de Covaleda trail or the surrounding Sierra de Urbión natural area. It's freely accessible for public enjoyment.

faqs.question: Where can I park to access the trailhead?
faqs.answer: While specific parking details aren't provided, trails like this in the Sierra de Urbión often have designated parking areas near the starting point, especially for popular routes. Look for signs for 'Ruta de las Cascadas' or 'Cascadas de Covaleda' near Covaleda village.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to visit the Cascadas de Covaleda for trail running?
faqs.answer: The trail is beautiful year-round, but spring and autumn are often ideal. In spring, the waterfalls are at their fullest, and the forests are lush. Autumn offers stunning fall foliage. Summers can be warm, so an early start is recommended. Winters can bring snow and ice, making some sections more challenging and potentially requiring specialized gear.

faqs.question: Is this trail suitable for families with children?
faqs.answer: While rated 'difficult' for trail running, the route is often considered low to medium difficulty for walking and is popular with families accustomed to hiking. The manageable length and well-signposted path make it accessible, but parents should be mindful of the steep and potentially slippery sections near the waterfalls.

faqs.question: Does this trail intersect with any other well-known paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route also passes through sections of the Covaleda-Pico Urbión trail, covering approximately 37% of its length. This offers opportunities to explore further if you wish to extend your adventure.

faqs.question: What should I wear and bring for trail running on this route?
faqs.answer: Given the varied terrain and potential for slippery sections, wear appropriate trail running shoes with good grip. Layers of clothing are advisable, as forest sections can be cooler. Bring enough water, especially on warmer days, and consider a small first-aid kit. Trekking poles can be helpful for stability on steeper descents.
